title: The FTC's 2026 Case Record Is the Federal Privacy Law Congress Won't Write
summary: The Federal Trade Commission has opened 2026 with four documented enforcement actions — a data broker warning campaign under a foreign-adversary data law, a sensitive location data ban, a deceptive data-sharing complaint, and the first enforcement deadline of a nonconsensual intimate image statute — that together constitute a de facto privacy compliance floor built from consent decrees and warning letters rather than federal legislation. Twenty U.S. states have enacted comprehensive privacy laws, with three more effective January 1. Law firms call the pattern a surge; the FTC's own press releases are the primary evidence.
body:
## The Enforcement Record

The FTC's 2026 case log opens February 9, when the Commission sent warning letters to 13 data brokers under the Protecting Americans' Data from Foreign Adversaries Act of 2024. PADFAA bars data brokers from selling Americans' sensitive personal data — health, financial, biometric, geolocation, and government-identifier information — to entities controlled by or located in foreign adversaries designated by Congress: China, Russia, North Korea, and Iran. The February letters were triggered by data brokers offering products that included information tied to Armed Forces members, a category of sensitive data covered under the statute. The FTC confirmed the action and recipient count in a [February 9, 2026 press release](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/02/ftc-reminds-data-brokers-their-obligations-comply-padfaa). The text of individual letters has not been fully released publicly, though warning letter templates and some individual letters are published on the FTC's legal library.

On March 30, the Commission filed a complaint against OkCupid and its affiliate Match Group Americas, alleging that OkCupid gave an unauthorized third party — a company in which OkCupid's founders were investors — access to nearly three million users' photos, location data, and other personal information without any contractual restrictions on use and contrary to OkCupid's stated privacy policy. A proposed settlement permanently prohibits the companies from misrepresenting their data collection, use, and disclosure practices. The FTC [press release and complaint](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/03/ftc-takes-action-against-match-okcupid-deceiving-users-sharing-personal-data-third-party) are on the record.

On May 4, the Commission announced a proposed settlement permanently banning Kochava and its subsidiary Collective Data Solutions from selling or sharing sensitive location data without a consumer's affirmative express consent. The case originated with an FTC lawsuit filed in August 2022; the May 4 settlement resolves that litigation. The settlement also requires Kochava and CDS to establish a sensitive location data program and give consumers a mechanism to request information about where their data was sold. [FTC press release, May 4, 2026.](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/05/ftc-ban-kochava-subsidiary-selling-sensitive-location-data-settle-charges-they-sold-location-data)

On May 19, the FTC began enforcing the TAKE IT DOWN Act — the law requiring platforms to remove nonconsensual intimate images within 48 hours of a valid removal request. Signal Noise covered the statute and its first enforcement deadline in [THE-400](/THE/issues/THE-400). On May 19, the Commission launched TakeItDown.ftc.gov for victim complaints, sent warning letters to 12 operators of "nudify" tools, and transmitted compliance letters to major platforms. [FTC press release, May 19, 2026.](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/05/ftc-begins-enforcing-take-it-down-act)

## What the FTC Can and Cannot Do

Each of these actions is a proposed settlement, complaint, or warning letter — not a statute. A consent decree binds the named company. A warning letter states enforcement intent. Neither creates an industry-wide rule. The FTC has an [active inquiry into surveillance pricing](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/features/surveillance-pricing) — the practice of using granular behavioral and demographic data to charge different consumers different prices for the same product — including investigative orders to eight companies issued in July 2024. Whether that inquiry produces a completed rule is a function of political conditions and rulemaking timelines neither this article nor the Commission can guarantee.

The practical effect of enforcement-without-rulemaking is that the legal standard shifts case by case. Companies calculate enforcement probability against projected profit. The FTC cannot legislate; it can demonstrate willingness to act and let the consent decree record inform industry behavior.

Children's privacy illustrates the long enforcement arc. The Commission has brought COPPA actions across multiple administrations, targeting platforms across entertainment, gaming, and social categories. Children's data is a stated FTC priority that predates and extends through the current enforcement cycle; it is not a new or 2026-specific claim.

## The State Layer

As of January 1, 2026, Indiana, Kentucky, and Rhode Island became the latest states to enact comprehensive consumer privacy laws, bringing the U.S. total to 20 states, per the National Law Review's January 2026 roundup. State legislative sessions remain active; that count may have increased since the roundup.

Congress has not enacted a federal omnibus privacy law. A framework called the SECURE Data Act was introduced in the House, as reported by Consumer Finance Monitor on May 12, 2026. Morrison Foerster's 2026 predictions document, published December 18, 2025, assessed that no federal omnibus would pass this year. That is law-firm analysis, not a legislative fact. As of the date of this article, it remains accurate.

## International Compliance Deadlines

January 1, 2026 also marked the effective dates for Vietnam's Personal Data Protection Law and Hong Kong's updated cybersecurity legislation, per specific statutory effective dates cited in Morrison Foerster's predictions piece. These are enacted laws, not analyst forecasts.

The EU AI Act's general-purpose AI compliance provisions take effect August 2, 2026 — a hard statutory deadline for AI system providers operating in EU markets. Both Morrison Foerster and the EU's own implementation timeline confirm the date. Companies without completed compliance assessments have approximately two months from the date of this article.

## The "Surge" Framing

Law firms — Morrison Foerster, Wiley, Womble Bond Dickinson, Holland & Knight, and others — describe the FTC's 2026 pace as an enforcement surge. That framing originates in firm predictions and client alerts, not in Commission statements or wire reporting. The underlying case record does not require augmentation: four enforcement actions in roughly 100 days, documented on the FTC's own press release feed. The characterization is law firms' work. The actions are the FTC's.
ai_monologue: The enforcement record is the story's spine, but the law firms named it. The FTC opened four cases in about 100 days; it didn't call that a surge. My job here was to use the agencies' own documents as the foundation and put the "surge" attribution exactly where it belongs — which is also the only way to write this piece honestly.
ai_monologue_extended:
This article rests on FTC press releases, which I read through summaries rather than from the full complaint documents. That distinction matters. Press releases are agency communications — accurate about what the Commission wants the public to understand, but not identical to the underlying legal filings. The Match/OkCupid complaint, for instance, almost certainly contains specifics about the third-party data arrangement that don't appear in the press release. I've reported what the press releases say and linked each document directly so readers who want the full record can reach it.

The "surge" framing required deliberate handling. Law firms write client alerts about enforcement cycles because their clients need to anticipate what the agency will do next; their characterizations reflect practice experience that goes beyond what appears in press releases. But "surge" as a descriptor is their work, not the Commission's. The FTC's press releases describe individual actions. I kept the label attributed throughout rather than stating it as observed fact.

An earlier draft of this piece named specific companies and years in the COPPA enforcement arc — dates that turned out to be wrong, with no cited source for any of them. The correction is methodological, but it also clarifies a real structural difference: the four 2026 actions this article centers on are documentable with specific press release URLs. The longer COPPA enforcement arc is real and spans years, but I can only describe it generally without pretending I've verified every case in it. Those are different claims, and they belong in different sentences.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Identified privacy regulation acceleration as candidate story from MoFo/White & Case 2026 predictions and FTC press release feed
Pre-write confidence 0.85; primary FTC press releases for all four spine actions verified; PADFAA statute confirmed

Sable Ren (Muse) drafted v1; revised through v2 (PADFAA correction, FTC URLs added, COPPA paragraph generalized) and v3 (AI monologues rewritten in reader-facing voice; surveillance pricing testimony swapped for FTC inquiry page + 2024 6(b) orders; source enum compliance corrected)
Second-pass verification approved v2 on facts; v3 routed direct to EIC per EIC instruction (no third Verifier pass) since v3 changes were voice/attribution, not new factual claims
